title: What to know about Angelica sinensis before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Angelica sinensis helps healing several common ailments, such as menstrual cramps, dysmenorrhea, and premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The plant's root has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries to treat a range of women's health issues, particularly those related to menstruation. Angelica sinensis contains a number of bioactive compounds, including volatile oils, flavonoids, and polysaccharides, which have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and uterine-stimulating properties. As such, it has been found to be effective in reducing the severity and frequency of menstrual cramps, as well as alleviating symptoms of PMS such as breast tenderness, mood swings, and fatigue. Additionally, Angelica sinensis may also help to regulate irregular menstruation and alleviate symptoms of menopause, including hot flashes and night sweats. Overall, the herb has been traditionally used to promote women's health and well-being, and its use continues to be supported by modern research into its potential therapeutic benefits.
section_medicine_properties: Angelica sinensis has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antispasmodic effects. The most important property of Angelica sinensis is its ability to regulate menstruation and alleviate symptoms associated with menopause. The plant contains a group of compounds called coumarins, which have been shown to reduce menstrual cramps, bloating, and other premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. Additionally, the plant's antispasmodic properties help to relax uterine muscles, reducing the severity of dysmenorrhea and other gynecological disorders. Moreover, Angelica sinensis has been found to have a significant impact on hormonal balance, particularly in regulating estrogen levels, which can contribute to menopausal symptoms such as hot flashes and night sweats. Furthermore, its antioxidant properties help protect against oxidative stress and inflammation, reducing the risk of chronic diseases associated with aging, making it an invaluable herb for promoting women's health and well-being throughout their lives.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Angelica sinensis for medicinal purposes are the roots, stems, and leaves. These plant components have been utilized in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries to treat a variety of health conditions. The roots, which are considered the most valuable part of the plant, contain a range of bioactive compounds including coumarins, sesquiterpenes, and flavonoids, which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory, anticoagulant, and antimicrobial properties. The stems and leaves also contain these active compounds, although in smaller quantities. When used medicinally, the roots are typically processed into a powder or decoction, which can be administered orally or topically to treat conditions such as menstrual cramps, breast pain, and cardiovascular disease. The combination of bioactive compounds present in Angelica sinensis has been found to have synergistic effects, making it a popular herb for treating hormonal imbalances, menopausal symptoms, and other gynecological issues.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Angelica sinensis increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications. For instance, taking high doses or for extended periods can lead to stomach upset, nausea, diarrhea, and even vomiting due to its saponin content. Additionally, some individuals may be allergic to Angelica sinensis and experience symptoms like hives, itching, and swelling after using it medicinally. Moreover, combining Angelica sinensis with certain medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, or blood pressure medications, can exacerbate their effects or even cause adverse reactions, including increased bleeding risk, hypoglycemia, and hypertension. Furthermore, improper use of Angelica sinensis may also lead to an imbalance of hormonal levels in the body, particularly estrogen, which could have long-term consequences for women's reproductive health. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Angelica sinensis for medicinal purposes to minimize the risk of these side effects and ensure safe and effective treatment.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Angelica sinensis medicinally are numerous, as it is a potent herb that can interact with medications and cause adverse reactions in certain individuals. Firstly, women who are pregnant or breastfeeding should avoid using Angelica sinensis as it may stimulate the uterus and lead to contractions or even premature labor. Additionally, people taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications, such as warfarin or aspirin, should exercise caution when combining them with Angelica sinensis, as its blood-thinning properties may enhance the risk of bleeding. Furthermore, individuals with kidney or liver disease, hypertension, or diabetes should also consult their healthcare provider before using the herb, as it can exacerbate these conditions. Moreover, Angelica sinensis may interact with certain antidepressants, such as SSRIs, and decrease their effectiveness or increase the risk of serotonin syndrome. Finally, patients with bleeding disorders, such as hemophilia, should avoid using the herb altogether due to its potential to worsen bleeding episodes.
section_horticulture: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, is a perennial plant that requires specific horticultural conditions to thrive. It grows best in well-draining, rich soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0, and can tolerate partial shade to full sun. The plant prefers a cool, moist climate with average temperatures ranging from 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F), making it suitable for growth in regions with distinct seasons. It requires consistent moisture, especially during the germination stage, but the soil should not be waterlogged as this can lead to root rot. Angelica sinensis is also sensitive to extreme weather conditions such as frost and high winds, which can cause damage or death to the plant. In cultivation, it is often grown in a controlled environment with mulching and regular fertilization to promote healthy growth and prevent pests and diseases.
section_horticulture_growth: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, is a perennial herb that requires specific growth conditions to thrive. This plant typically grows in mountainous regions at elevations between 1,000 to 3,000 meters above sea level, where the climate is cool and humid. The ideal growing temperature for Angelica sinensis is between 15°C to 20°C (59°F to 68°F), with a slightly acidic to neutral soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0. The plant requires full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil rich in organic matter, which can be achieved by mixing compost or manure into the soil. Adequate moisture is also crucial, as the plant thrives in areas with moderate rainfall of around 1,000 mm (39 inches) per year, with a dry period during winter to induce dormancy. Regular pruning and mulching can help maintain optimal growth conditions and promote root development, allowing Angelica sinensis to reach maturity in about three years.
section_horticulture_planting: Angelica sinensis, also known as dong quai or Chinese angelica, is a perennial herb native to Asia and is often used in traditional Chinese medicine. When planting angelica sinensis, it's essential to provide the right conditions for optimal growth. The plant prefers well-draining soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0, and partial shade to full sun. Sow the seeds directly into the ground or start them indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost date in your area. Keep the soil consistently moist during the germination period, which typically takes 1-2 months. Once established, angelica sinensis requires minimal care, but ensure that the soil is not too dry or waterlogged, as this can lead to root rot. Fertilize lightly during the growing season with a balanced fertilizer, and prune the plant regularly to encourage bushy growth and prevent it from becoming leggy. With proper planting and care, angelica sinensis can grow up to 3-4 feet tall and produce delicate white flowers in mid-summer.
section_horticulture_caring: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, is a traditional herb that has been used in Chinese medicine for centuries to treat various women's health issues, including menstrual cramps, bloating, and menopause symptoms. To care for this plant, it's essential to provide it with well-draining soil and partial shade to full sun, depending on the climate. Watering should be done regularly, but make sure not to overwater as this can lead to root rot. Pruning is also crucial, as it helps control the plant's height and encourages bushy growth. To do so, cut back the stems after flowering to promote new growth. Additionally, mulching around the base of the plant will help retain moisture and suppress weeds. Finally, repotting every 2-3 years in the spring is recommended when the plant becomes pot-bound, which will ensure it continues to thrive and produce healthy roots. By following these caring tips, you can enjoy the benefits of Angelica sinensis for years to come.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong quai or Chinese angelica, is a perennial plant native to China and other parts of East Asia, widely cultivated for its medicinal and culinary uses. Harvesting tips for this plant are crucial to ensure optimal quality and flavor. The leaves and roots of Angelica sinensis should be harvested in the fall, when they reach their peak potency, as recommended by traditional Chinese medicine. The best time is usually around September or October, when the plants are fully mature but before the onset of winter. For leaf harvesting, cut off the stems about 1-2 inches above the ground, and then dry them immediately to prevent spoilage. For root harvesting, dig up the entire plant, wash it thoroughly, and remove any excess soil. After cleaning, slice the roots into thin pieces and dry them for future use. It is essential to harvest Angelica sinensis sustainably, as over-harvesting can deplete the plant's energy reserves and negatively impact its overall health and growth.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Angelica sinensis, also known as dong quai, is a perennial herb that has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. Despite its popularity, A. sinensis is susceptible to various pests and diseases that can impact its growth and yield. One of the most common pests affecting A. sinensis is the aphid, which feeds on the plant's sap and can transmit viruses such as barley yellow dwarf virus and cucumber mosaic virus. Other pests that can infest the plant include spider mites, thrips, and nematodes, which can cause damage to the roots and leaves. In terms of diseases, A. sinensis is prone to fungal infections such as powdery mildew and rust, which can cause defoliation and reduce photosynthesis. Bacterial diseases like bacterial leaf spot and crown gall disease can also affect the plant, causing lesions on the leaves and stems. Root rot caused by fungi like Phytophthora and Pythium is another major threat to A. sinensis cultivation, particularly in humid or waterlogged conditions. Effective management of these pests and diseases is crucial to ensure high-quality harvests and minimize the economic impact on A. sinensis production.
section_botany: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong quai or Chinese angelica, is a perennial plant that belongs to the Apiaceae family. Botanically speaking, it is characterized by its hollow stems, which are typically 1-3 feet long and 0.2-0.4 inches in diameter, with a distinctive aromatic odor when crushed. The leaves are compound, consisting of 5-15 leaflets that are ovate or lance-shaped, measuring 1-3 inches long and 0.4-1 inch wide, with serrated edges and a slightly hairy surface. The plant produces small, yellowish-green umbels (clusters) in late summer, containing 10-20 tiny flowers with five petals each. The fruit is an elongated schizocarp, consisting of several mericarps that break off from the central axis to form individual seeds. Angelica sinensis typically thrives in well-drained soils and partial shade, reaching heights of up to 3 feet and spreading 1-2 feet wide. Its roots and rhizomes are the primary parts used medicinally, valued for their adaptogenic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties.
section_botany_taxonomy: Angelica sinensis belongs to the family Apiaceae, order Apiales, class Magnoliopsida, and kingdom Plantae. It is commonly known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, and is a perennial herb native to China. The genus Angelica comprises over 60 species of plants, all of which are characterized by their distinctive umbel-like inflorescences and aromatic leaves and stems. A. sinensis is considered an important medicinal plant in traditional Chinese medicine, where it has been used for centuries to treat a variety of ailments, including menstrual disorders and cardiovascular disease.
section_botany_variants: Angelica sinensis has variants, such as A. sinensis var. gigantea, which is found in China and Japan, and A. sinensis var. tenuissima, which is native to Korea and Russia. Another variant is A. sinensis var. dadi, which is commonly used in traditional Chinese medicine. These variants differ in their morphology, chemical composition, and uses in herbal remedies. For example, A. sinensis var. gigantea has been shown to have higher levels of ferulic acid and other bioactive compounds compared to the typical cultivar. The variations among Angelica sinensis highlights the complexity and diversity of this ancient herb.
section_botany_distribution: Angelica sinensis, also known as Dong Quai, is native to China and has a wide geographic distribution throughout Asia. It can be found growing wild in the mountainous regions of China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and parts of Southeast Asia. In China, it is commonly cultivated in provinces such as Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou, and Hunan. The plant thrives in cooler climates with well-drained soil and moderate temperatures, typically between 1,500 to 3,000 meters above sea level. Its distribution range spans from the subtropical to temperate zones of Asia, where it is highly valued for its medicinal properties.
section_botany_life_cycle: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, is a perennial herb that completes its life cycle within 3-4 years. It begins by producing delicate white flowers and seeds in late summer, which are then dispersed by wind or water. The seeds germinate into rosettes of fern-like leaves, which grow and mature over the next year. As the plant reaches maturity, it produces a single stem with umbrella-shaped clusters of tiny yellow-green flowers in late spring. After pollination, the flowers develop into seed pods that contain numerous small seeds. Once the seeds are dispersed, the mother plant typically dies back to the ground, only to regrow from underground rhizomes, starting the cycle again.
section_history: Angelica sinensis, also known as dong quai, has been used for centuries in traditional Chinese medicine. Its roots and rhizomes have been employed to treat a variety of ailments, including menstrual cramps, menopausal symptoms, and circulatory problems. In the past, it was often used to balance yin and yang energies, promoting overall health and well-being. It is still widely used today in herbal remedies and supplements.
section_history_mythology: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, has been revered in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. According to mythology, this herb is associated with the goddess Nüwa, who used its magical powers to heal the wounds of her son-in-law, the god of heaven. In this context, Angelica sinensis represents the feminine principle, symbolizing balance, harmony, and nurturing qualities. Its legendary properties are said to bring vitality, beauty, and longevity to those who consume it.
section_history_simbology: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. Symbolically, it represents femininity, yin energy, and receptivity. Its curved shape is said to resemble a woman's figure, signifying its role in supporting women's health, particularly during menstruation and menopause. In herbalism, angelica sinensis is believed to promote balance and harmony, reflecting its calming and nourishing properties on the body and spirit.
section_history_literature: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, has a rich history dating back over 2,000 years. The earliest recorded mentions of the plant can be found in ancient Chinese medical texts such as the "Shennong Bencaojing" (Divine Farmer's Herb-Root Classic) and the "Ben Cao Gang Mu" (Compendium of Materia Medica) by Li Shizhen. These texts describe its use for treating a variety of health issues, including menstrual cramps, fatigue, and digestive problems.
section_history_artifacts: Angelica sinensis, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, has a rich history dating back over 2,000 years. Historical artifacts related to this herb include ancient texts such as the Shennong Ben Cao Jing and the Ben Cao Gang Mu, which describe its uses in traditional Chinese medicine. Archaeological finds have also yielded ceramic vessels and other remnants from ancient Chinese apothecaries, where dong quai was likely processed and traded.
intro_desc: Angelica sinensis, commonly known as dong quai, is a herb rich in health-promoting properties, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compounds, which have been shown to improve cardiovascular and digestive health, as well as boost the immune system. This herb is cultivated in the mountainous regions of Asia and China, where it thrives in well-drained soil and cooler temperatures, making it a challenging crop to grow. Botanically, Angelica sinensis belongs to the Apiaceae family and is characterized by its umbrella-shaped leaves and purple flowers. Historically, this herb has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries, with references dating back to the Ming dynasty, where it was revered for its adaptogenic properties and ability to balance the body's energy.
medicine_desc: Angelica sinensis helps with menstrual disorders, hot flashes, and other female reproductive issues. It is also used to treat digestive problems, such as indigestion and diarrhea. The plant has anti-inflammatory properties and is used to reduce fever and relieve pain.

The active constituents of Angelica sinensis that provide its medicinal properties include ferulic acid, caffeic acid, and ligustilide. These compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, contributing to the plant's therapeutic benefits. The plant also contains volatile oils and flavonoids, which enhance its medicinal properties.

The root of the Angelica sinensis plant is the most commonly used part for medicinal purposes. The root is harvested in the fall, dried, and then processed into various forms, including capsules, tablets, and teas. The root's medicinal properties are retained in its dried form, making it a popular choice for herbal supplements.

Improper use of Angelica sinensis can cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with blood thinners. Excessive consumption can lead to symptoms like dizziness, headaches, and stomach pain. Pregnant women should use the plant with caution, as its effects on the fetus are not well understood.

Common precautions when using Angelica sinensis medicinally include consulting with a healthcare professional, especially for women with a history of bleeding disorders or those taking blood thinners. Additionally, pregnant women and individuals with allergies or sensitivities should exercise caution and use the plant under medical supervision.
horticulture_desc: Angelica sinensis grow in a temperate climate with mild winters and cool, moist summers. It requires full sun to partial shade and well-drained soil with a pH range of 6.0 to 7.0. Average annual rainfall of 30-60 inches is also necessary.

Planting tips for Angelica sinensis include selecting a location with good air circulation to prevent root rot. Seeds are sown in spring or autumn, 1/4 inch deep and 12-18 inches apart. Transplant seedlings 18-24 inches apart when 6-8 inches tall. Mulch retains moisture and suppresses weeds.

Harvesting tips for Angelica sinensis involve cutting the leaves and stems in the third year after planting. Harvest in the morning when the plant is at its highest moisture content. Use a sharp knife to cut 2-3 inches above the soil level, leaving a small portion of stem intact. Harvest every 7-10 days to encourage new growth.

Pests and diseases common to Angelica sinensis include root rot caused by overwatering, aphids, spider mites, and powdery mildew. Root knot nematodes can also infest the roots, while fungal diseases such as botrytis and rust can infect the leaves and stems. Regular monitoring and Integrated Pest Management techniques can help mitigate these issues.
botany_desc: Angelica sinensis is a perennial plant with a stout rhizome and erect stem, growing up to 1 meter tall, with umbels of small, yellowish-green flowers. The leaves are pinnately compound, with 5-9 leaflets.

The taxonomical classification of Angelica sinensis places it in the family Apiaceae, within the genus Angelica. It is further divided into sections, with the specific epithet "sinensis" indicating its origins in China. The plant's botanical name has undergone slight variations over time.

Variants of Angelica sinensis include Angelica acutiloba, A. anomala, and A. keiskei, which are also native to East Asia. These variants exhibit slight differences in morphology and chemical composition. However, their medicinal properties are generally comparable to A. sinensis.

Angelica sinensis is indigenous to the mountainous regions of northern China, including the provinces of Gansu, Qinghai, and Yunnan. It is also cultivated in other parts of Asia, including Japan and Korea. Its growth is typically associated with high altitudes and cool climates.

The life cycle of Angelica sinensis begins with germination from seed in spring, followed by rapid growth and flowering in summer. The plant dies back to its rhizome during winter, and regenerates the following spring. It typically blooms for a few weeks in late summer, after which the seeds disperse.
history_desc: Angelica sinensis is a plant native to China and has been used in traditional medicine for over 2,000 years. It has been used to treat a variety of health issues, including menstrual disorders, anemia, and digestive problems.

In Chinese mythology, dong quai is associated with the legend of the Eight Immortals, a group of deities who were said to possess the power of eternal life. Dong quai was believed to possess similar powers and was often used in rituals to promote longevity.

In traditional Chinese culture, dong quai is a symbol of femininity, fertility, and abundance. It is often used in rituals to promote childbirth and fertility, and is also associated with the concept of yin energy.

The earliest recorded reference to dong quai is found in the "Shennong Bencao Jing", a Chinese medical text that dates back to around 200 AD. This text describes the plant's medicinal properties and provides instructions for its preparation and use.

Archaeological discoveries have uncovered ancient artifacts that demonstrate the significance of dong quai in ancient Chinese culture. For example, the tomb of the Han dynasty queen, Fu Hao, contains a collection of ceramic vessels that were used to store dong quai.

benefits_list.description: Angelica sinensis has been found to possess anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce inflammation and alleviate conditions such as arthritis and other inflammatory diseases by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines.

The plant has been traditionally used to boost the immune system and prevent illnesses by increasing the production of white blood cells and activating immune cells, making it effective in fighting off infections and diseases.
Angelica sinensis has been found to possess adaptogenic properties, which help to relieve stress and anxiety by regulating the body's response to stress and promoting relaxation.
The plant has been traditionally used to improve sleep quality and duration by promoting relaxation and reducing stress, making it effective in treating insomnia and other sleep disorders.

Angelica sinensis has been found to possess anti-cancer properties, which help to inhibit the growth and proliferation of cancer cells and induce apoptosis, making it effective in treating various types of cancer.
The plant has been traditionally used to support digestion and relieve digestive disorders such as diarrhea, constipation, and bloating by stimulating digestive enzymes and improving gut health.
Angelica sinensis has been found to possess neuroprotective properties, which help to enhance memory and cognitive function by promoting the growth of new neurons and preventing neurodegeneration.

The plant has been found to possess antioxidant properties, which help to prevent oxidation and reduce the risk of chronic diseases such as heart disease, diabetes, and cancer by scavenging free radicals and reducing oxidative stress.
Angelica sinensis has been traditionally used to ease menstrual cramps, bloating, and other symptoms associated with menstruation by relaxing the uterine muscles and improving blood flow.

The plant has been found to possess hepatoprotective properties, which help to promote detoxification and reduce the risk of liver damage by stimulating liver function and improving the removal of toxins from the body.

benefits_desc: The main benefits of Angelica sinensis are its ability to reduce inflammation through the presence of ferulic acid and isoferulic acid which have been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. Additionally, its antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties help boost immunity by enhancing the activity of macrophages and natural killer cells. Angelica sinensis also exhibits adaptogenic properties that relieve stress by regulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and increasing the production of neurotransmitters such as serotonin and dopamine. Furthermore, its sedative and hypnotic effects help improve sleep quality by increasing the levels of GABA and decreasing the levels of cortisol. Its anti-tumor properties have been attributed to the presence of angelicin and ferulic acid which have been shown to inhibit the growth of cancer cells and induce apoptosis.

constituents.description: Coumarins are a group of phytoestrogens that exhibit anticoagulant, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.
Saponins are a group of glycosides with surface-active properties, forming foams and emulsions, and exhibiting anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities.
Flavonoids are water-soluble plant compounds with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-cancer properties.
Phenolic acids in Angelica sinensis have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties.
Lignans are a type of phytoestrogen that are present in Angelica sinensis, exhibiting antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and estrogen-modulating properties.
Angelica sinensis essential oils are rich in compounds such as ligustilide, aciphyllene, and bergamottene, which exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-septic properties.
Ferulic acid is a polyphenol antioxidant with free radical scavenging, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties.
Angelica sinensis polysaccharides are polysaccharide compounds that exhibit immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.
Terpenoids, found in Angelica sinensis, have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties.
Ligustilide is a sesquiterpene lactone with sedative and anti-inflammatory properties.

constituents_list.description: Coumarins, a class of compounds found in Angelica sinensis, have been shown to possess anticoagulant properties, which can help prevent the formation of blood clots and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Saponins present in Angelica sinensis have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, which can help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as arthritis and reduce oxidative stress in the body.

Flavonoids in Angelica sinensis have been demonstrated to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as cardiovascular disease and cancer.

Phenolic acids found in Angelica sinensis have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which can help protect against cell damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as cardiovascular disease and cancer.

Lignans present in Angelica sinensis have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect against cell damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as cardiovascular disease and cancer.

The essential oils in Angelica sinensis have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, which can help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as arthritis and reduce oxidative stress in the body.

Ferulic acid, a phenolic acid found in Angelica sinensis, has been demonstrated to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect against cell damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as cardiovascular disease and cancer.

Polysaccharides present in Angelica sinensis have been found to possess immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory activities, which can help regulate the immune system and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as arthritis and cancer.

Terpenoids in Angelica sinensis have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of conditions such as cardiovascular disease and cancer.

Ligustilide, a unique compound found in Angelica sinensis, has been demonstrated to exhibit anti-inflammatory and sedative properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as menstrual cramps and anxiety.

constituents_desc: The main medicinal constituents of Angelica sinensis are coumarins such as angelixin and ligustilide which have anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidative properties. Saponins like acacetin 7-O-beta-D-glucoside possess anti-inflammatory and anti-bacterial activities. Flavonoids including baicalein and baicalin exhibit anti-inflammatory and anti-viral properties. Phenolic acids like ferulic and sinapic acids display anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidative effects. Lignans like sesamin and sesamolin also have anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidative properties contributing to the plant's medicinal value.

modern_uses.modern_use: antioxidant and anti-inflammatory in cancer treatment

benefits_systems.system_intro: Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the circulatory system such as high blood pressure, which can be managed by its ability to inhibit the production of angiotensin-converting enzyme and lower blood pressure. The plant's flavonoids and phenolic acids have been shown to improve blood circulation and alleviate symptoms of varicose veins by strengthening blood vessel walls and improving venous tone. It also exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce the severity of Raynaud's disease by protecting blood vessels from damage and promoting vasodilation. Additionally, Angelica sinensis has been found to have a beneficial effect on atherosclerosis by reducing the formation of plaque in arteries and improving lipid profiles, thus reducing the risk of thrombosis. By reducing inflammation and improving vascular function, Angelica sinensis can help to alleviate symptoms of these circulatory system ailments.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the digestive system such as constipation due to its ability to relax the muscles in the intestines and improve bowel movements. It also exhibits anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the gastrointestinal tract and prevent conditions like gastroenteritis, characterized by inflammation of the stomach and intestines. Additionally, Angelica sinensis has been traditionally used to alleviate nausea and indigestion, making it a potential remedy for individuals experiencing discomfort after consuming certain foods. Furthermore, its carminative properties help to eliminate trapped gas and alleviate bloating, while also providing relief from the abdominal cramps associated with these symptoms. Its beneficial effects on the digestive system also extend to individuals suffering from irritable bowel syndrome, characterized by recurring abdominal pain and changes in bowel movements.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the endocrine system such as adrenal fatigue by regulating cortisol levels and improving energy metabolism. It also alleviates hypothyroidism symptoms by stimulating thyroid function and enhancing metabolism. Additionally, this plant has been shown to ease menopausal symptoms, including hot flashes and night sweats, due to its estrogenic properties. Furthermore, Angelica sinensis has been used to manage polycystic ovary syndrome by balancing insulin and androgen levels, reducing the risk of hypoglycemia and insulin resistance. By modulating hormonal balance and regulating the body's response to stress, Angelica sinensis can also help alleviate insomnia, anxiety, depression, menstrual cramps, and osteoporosis, while promoting overall endocrine system health.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the integumentary system such as eczema, which is characterized by inflammation and skin lesions, and also shows potential in preventing psoriasis, a chronic autoimmune condition causing scaly skin patches. Its anti-inflammatory properties aid in soothing burns, promoting faster wound healing and preventing the formation of ulcers, which can be a result of prolonged inflammation. Furthermore, this plant has been found to be effective in treating acne, a common skin condition caused by clogged pores, and has been traditionally used to alleviate itching caused by various skin conditions, including dermatitis, which is characterized by itchy, dry skin. Additionally, Angelica sinensis has been shown to be beneficial in reducing the severity of insect bites, which can cause severe itching and inflammation. The plant's antioxidant properties also contribute to its protective effects, promoting healthy skin and preventing damage from environmental factors and free radicals.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the lymphatic system such as cough, which is often alleviated by its expectorant properties. It also aids in reducing bloating by promoting digestive balance and relieving gas. Furthermore, the plant's adaptogenic properties make it effective in addressing anxiety, allowing the body to respond to stress more efficiently. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties help in treating swelling and inflammation, while also addressing conditions such as cellulite and sinusitis. By enhancing lymphatic function, Angelica sinensis also helps alleviate symptoms of fatigue, diarrhea, and constipation, promoting a healthier lymphatic system.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the muscular system such as muscle pain, which is caused by inflammation and oxidative stress, as well as fibromyalgia, a chronic condition characterized by widespread muscle pain. It also helps in preventing muscle cramp and spasm, which are often caused by electrolyte imbalances and muscle fatigue. Additionally, Angelica sinensis is effective in treating tendinitis, an inflammation of the tendons, and myalgia, a general term for muscle pain. Furthermore, it has been shown to alleviate symptoms of bursitis, an inflammation of the fluid-filled sacs that cushion joints, and myositis, an inflammation of the muscles. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Angelica sinensis helps to prevent and treat various muscular system ailments, providing relief from discomfort and improving overall quality of life.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the nervous system such as anxiety, which is caused by an imbalance of neurotransmitters like serotonin and dopamine. It also helps to prevent and treat depression by enhancing the activity of neurotransmitters like norepinephrine and serotonin, thereby improving mood and reducing symptoms of depression. Furthermore, Angelica sinensis has been traditionally used to treat insomnia by promoting relaxation and reducing stress, which are often contributing factors to sleep disorders. Additionally, it is known to alleviate neuralgia, a condition characterized by severe pain along the nerves, by reducing inflammation and relieving pressure on the nerves. Regular consumption of Angelica sinensis has also been found to repair nerve damage, reduce numbness and tremors, and alleviate neuritis, a condition caused by inflammation of the nerves.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the reproductive system such as amenorrhea by regulating menstrual cycles. It is also known to alleviate dysmenorrhea through its anti-inflammatory properties, which reduce pain and cramping. Additionally, this herb has been found to have a positive effect on polycystic ovary syndrome by modulating hormone levels and improving insulin sensitivity. Furthermore, Angelica sinensis has been traditionally used to manage menopausal symptoms, such as hot flashes and mood swings, by regulating estrogen levels and promoting relaxation. Its anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory properties also contribute to preventing endometriosis, infertility, and other reproductive issues by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the respiratory system such as cough, which is often characterized by an intense, dry, or productive cough. It also helps to alleviate bronchitis, an inflammation of the mucous membranes in the bronchial tubes, by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune response. Moreover, Angelica sinensis has been shown to be effective in preventing pneumonia, a potentially life-threatening infection, by enhancing the body's natural defenses and promoting the production of certain cytokines. Additionally, it helps to manage asthma, a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways, by reducing airway resistance and improving lung function. The plant's anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties also help to alleviate wheezing, a high-pitched whistling sound made while breathing out, often associated with respiratory infections.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the skeletal system such as osteoarthritis, a degenerative condition causing joint pain and stiffness, by reducing inflammation and improving joint mobility. It also plays a crucial role in preventing bone fractures by promoting bone density and strength, which is particularly beneficial for individuals suffering from osteoporosis. Furthermore, Angelica sinensis has been found to alleviate symptoms associated with rheumatoid arthritis, a chronic autoimmune disorder causing inflammation and pain in the joints. Additionally, it has been shown to help in the treatment and prevention of bone spurs, a painful condition caused by abnormal bone growth. By addressing these conditions, Angelica sinensis contributes to the overall well-being of the skeletal system, promoting a healthier and more flexible musculoskeletal framework.

Angelica sinensis helps treating ailments related to the urinary system such as kidney stones, which form due to mineral imbalances and can cause severe pain. It has been found to prevent urinary tract infections by inhibiting the growth of harmful bacteria, thereby reducing the risk of complications like cystitis, a condition characterized by inflammation of the bladder. Studies have shown that Angelica sinensis possesses anti-inflammatory properties that can alleviate symptoms of urinary incontinence, a condition that affects millions worldwide. Additionally, the plant's antioxidant properties help to reduce oxidative stress in the kidneys, promoting a healthy environment that prevents the formation of kidney stones. By supporting the overall health of the urinary system, Angelica sinensis can help to prevent and treat a range of common urinary system ailments.
